title: Combining Dense and Sparse Scores
concept: Running both retrieval methods in parallel and merging their result sets for a single query.

exercises.prompt: What type of search queries do traditional dense vector embeddings struggle with the most?

options.text: Exact product codes or specific serial numbers

exercises.explain: To set up our journey into hybrid systems, let's look at why standard vector embeddings sometimes struggle. Sparse search excels at exact, precise matches like serial numbers or product IDs, which dense embeddings often fail to match perfectly.

cards.title: The Search Blindspot
cards.body: Have you ever searched for something and got zero results because you didn't use the exact magic words, even though the database had exactly what you needed? Hybrid search fixes this by pairing keyword matching with conceptual understanding.

cards.title: What is Hybrid Search?
cards.body: Hybrid search combines two different retrieval techniques: sparse search (which looks for exact matching words) and dense search (which understands deep concepts and synonyms) to find the absolute best results.

cards.title: The Dual-Expert Audition
cards.body: Imagine hiring both an art critic (dense search) and a keyword indexer (sparse search) to find the perfect painting. One looks for overall mood and emotion, while the other looks for specific objects like 'red umbrella'. Combining their scores gives you the best of both worlds.

cards.title: Hybrid Search in Action
cards.body: If you search for 'cellular device screen fix', sparse search finds documents with the exact words 'device' and 'fix', while dense search finds articles about 'repairing iPhone displays' even if they don't share those exact words.

cards.title: Vectors Don't Solve Everything
cards.body: Many think dense vector search makes keyword search obsolete. In reality, vector search often misses highly specific codes, part numbers, or rare brand names that keyword search excels at finding.

cards.title: Precision vs. Intuition
cards.body: Instead of choosing between precise words or abstract concepts, hybrid search merges both streams into a single ranked list.

left.text: Finds exact terms, serial numbers, and specific codes.

right.text: Finds synonyms, related topics, and general intent.

exercises.prompt: Why does hybrid search combine dense and sparse retrieval methods?

options.text: It only uses sparse keyword matching.
It completely ignores exact word matches.
It combines exact keyword matching with deep semantic understanding.
It converts text into images before searching.

exercises.explain: Hybrid search combines dense search (which captures semantic meaning and synonyms) with sparse search (which matches exact keywords and codes).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Running dense and sparse searches in parallel helps keep the overall search response time low.
exercises.explain: Running them in parallel is much faster than running them one after another, resulting in lower latency for the user.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how hybrid search pipelines execute their retrieval steps.
exercises.sentence: A hybrid search engine runs both retrieval methods in parallel to gather candidate documents quickly.
exercises.explain: To save time and get the complete picture, hybrid search executes both the keyword and semantic queries in parallel before merging the results.

exercises.prompt: Identify the incorrect statement about dense and sparse search.

options.text: Sparse search looks for exact keyword overlaps, while dense search uses vector math to find conceptually similar matches.
Sparse search understands the conceptual meaning of words, while dense search only matches exact character sequences.
Combining both dense and sparse scores allows a search engine to rank documents by both keyword matches and topic relevance.

exercises.explain: The second option is the only one that incorrectly describes sparse search as understanding 'conceptual meaning' and dense search as looking for 'exact character sequences', which is the exact opposite of how they work.

exercises.prompt: How does a hybrid search engine combine dense and sparse search capabilities for a single user query?

options.text: By converting all sparse keywords into dense vectors before running a single search query
By running both dense and sparse queries in parallel and merging their resulting score sets
By running sparse search first, and then using dense search only on the top 3 results
By discarding keyword scores entirely and relying only on vector similarity matches

exercises.explain: Hybrid search excels because it executes both sparse (keyword) and dense (semantic) queries in parallel, then combines their output scores. This ensures you do not miss exact matches while still capturing deep conceptual meanings.
